content: Just as human language learning of the term 'reflexion' builds layered semantic understanding through repeated contextual exposure and deliberate reflection on usage, LLM self-reflection mechanisms can borrow this structured, vocabulary-acquisition framework to organize their dynamic self-corrective memory: storing incorrect reasoning paths as 'wrong context' definitions and correct solutions as 'valid semantic entries' to improve future decision-making. This turns the arbitrary linguistic mapping of the term 'reflexion' into a blueprint for designing more structured reflective memory for autonomous agents.
